channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s Son Emerges as Leading Choice to Be His Successor
item.description: If Mojtaba Khamenei is chosen by Iran’s senior clerics, it may signal a victory for hard-liners, an analyst said.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Morteza Nikoubazl/NurPhoto, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mojtaba Khamenei, son of the slain former leader of Iran, in Tehran in 2019.

item.title: These American Service Members Died in the Iran Conflict
item.description: They were from Nebraska, Florida, Iowa and Minnesota. One was wrapping up his final deployment and hoped to open a martial arts studio.
item.dc:creator: Corina Knoll, Christina Morales, Pooja Salhotra and Susan C. Beachy
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: United States Army Reserve

item.title: Dan Crenshaw Loses to Steve Toth for Texas District 2
item.description: The victory by Steve Toth, a hard-line Texas state representative, underscored how even a conservative House member could lose Republican voters by breaking with President Trump.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Dan Crenshaw, Republican of Texas, faced a primary challenge from Steve Toth, one of the most conservative members of the Texas House.

item.title: Roy Cooper and Michael Whatley Advance in North Carolina Senate Race
item.description: North Carolina has broken national Democrats’ hearts for nearly two decades, but this time they hope that Roy Cooper, the former governor, can once again find a path to victory.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times; Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Roy Cooper, left, the former Democratic governor of North Carolina, and Michael Whatley, the former chairman of the Republican National Committee.

item.title: U.S. Takes Military Action in Ecuador Against ‘Terrorist Organizations’
item.description: U.S. Special Forces soldiers are advising and supporting Ecuadorian commandos on raids across the country against suspected drug shipment facilities and other drug-related sites.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Marcos Pin/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Ecuadorian Navy stand guard in the facilities of the Coast Guard Command at the Port of Manta, Ecuador, in 2025.

item.title: Lutnick Agrees to Testify in House Epstein Investigation
item.description: The Commerce Secretary, who misrepresented his relationship with the convicted sex offender, has volunteered to appear, the House Oversight Committee’s leader said.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Yuri Gripas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Howard Lutnick, commerce secretary, testifying before the Senate earlier this month.

item.title: Epstein, ‘Lolita’ and a Culture of Disembodiment
item.description: Disturbing images released in the Epstein files showing passages from Nabokov’s infamous novel written on bodies exemplify a world where women and girls are treated as objects for consumption.
item.dc:creator: Rhonda Garelick

item.title: An Epstein Associate Steps Down From Japanese Government Tech Project
item.description: Joichi Ito’s involvement in a publicly funded Japanese initiative had come under scrutiny after new details revealed his close relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis and Hisako Ueno
item.media:credit: Kimberly White/Getty Images
item.media:description: Joichi Ito is leaving the Global Startup Campus Initiative after renewed scrutiny over his past ties to the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Where are China’s A.I. Doomers?
item.description: Chinese policymakers and the public have expressed high levels of optimism about A.I., even as many in the West worry about the technology’s effects on employment or humanity in general.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Wang
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Service robots on display at the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai last year.

item.title: Billionaires Spending More Than Ever on Miami Real Estate
item.description: The ultrawealthy are vying for a limited number of exclusive properties on the islands and shorelines of South Florida.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Discerning buyers want waterfront properties, with plenty of land and unobstructed views of downtown Miami.

item.title: Trump Immigration Sweeps Spawn Underground Safe Houses
item.description: In Springfield, Ohio, some Americans have converted their basements and spare bedrooms into shelters for immigrant families who could be targeted in raids.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan and Maddie McGarvey
item.media:description: A spare bedroom in a Springfield, Ohio, house prepared for Haitians, in case the immigrants didn’t feel safe in their own homes.

item.title: ICE Officials Must Acknowledge Court Orders in Writing, Judge Says
item.description: Judge Michael E. Farbiarz warned in an order on Monday that he may have to hold the Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency in criminal contempt.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters at an immigration detention center in New Jersey. Several federal judges are grappling with how to address a pattern of government noncompliance in immigration cases.

item.title: ‘Cheap’ and ‘Appalling’: Trump’s Ballroom Plans Receive a Flood of Negative Comments
item.description: The National Capital Planning Commission received about 32,000 messages during its public comment period. Suffice it to say: Many people are not happy with the president’s ballroom plans.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Dylan Freedman
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s demolition of the East Wing and remaking of the White House in his own style have struck a nerve with Americans.

item.title: What to Know About Nepal's Gen Z Election
item.description: Nepal is holding its first election since a youth-led uprising against corruption toppled the government last year. Here is what to know.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech and Binod Ghimire
item.media:credit: Hannah Reyes Morales for The New York Times
item.media:description: People attending a campaign rally in Kathmandu, Nepal, on Saturday.

item.title: Big Lenders’ Risky Loans Are Rattling Wall Street
item.description: Lending troubles at Blue Owl Capital and other so-called private credit behemoths are setting off fears of a “bank run,” as one hedge fund put it.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Maureen Farrell
item.media:credit: Chris O'Meara/Associated Press
item.media:description: Doug Ostrover, a founder of Blue Owl Capital. Investors are worried that colossal “private credit” lenders like Blue Owl have been too optimistic in their assessments of multiyear, privately traded loans tied to risky companies and industries.

item.title: MrBeast Is Getting Into Financial Services With His Step App. Parents, Pay Attention.
item.description: Beast Industries has acquired a banklike app for young people and could eventually offer a variety of financial services, including crypto.
item.dc:creator: Tara Siegel Bernard and Ron Lieber
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jimmy Donaldson, 27, better known as MrBeast, has a large following of teenagers and young adults. He has said he wants to provide them with the financial basics he lacked when he was younger.

item.title: Chimpanzees Are Really Into Crystals
item.description: In an attempt to understand our own fascination with the shiny minerals, researchers gave some to chimps.
item.dc:creator: Cara Giaimo

item.title: Hot Air Balloon Rescue in Texas After It Strikes a Cell Tower
item.description: Two people were left dangling from a communications tower near Longview, Texas, on Saturday until more than a dozen firefighters could scale the equivalent of a skyscraper to reach them.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan

item.title: Can Owning a Pet Help You Live Longer?
item.description: Research suggests pets keep you healthier. But there are some caveats.
item.dc:creator: Dana G. Smith
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thanks to his dog, Rumi, Dr. Dhruv Kazi says he spends more time outside and has gotten to know his neighbors — and their dogs.

item.title: There Is Nothing Inevitable About a Russian Victory in Ukraine
item.description: If Russia has all the cards, why has it achieved so little?
item.dc:creator: Lawrence Freedman
item.media:credit: Oksana Parafeniuk for The New York Times

item.title: We Are Finally Free From Khamenei’s Suffocating Gaze
item.description: Now that Ayatollah Khamenei is dead, will another face replace his and carry on a version of the same story?
item.dc:creator: Azadeh Moaveni
item.media:credit: Abbas/Magnum Photos

item.title: Toronto Synagogue Is Damaged by Gunfire on Night of Jewish Holiday
item.description: The temple said 20 shots were fired at the building but no one was hurt.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk

item.title: Vail Resorts Reduces the Price of Its Epic Pass for Gen Z Skiers
item.description: Seeming to acknowledge critics’ complaints about the high cost of snow sports, the company is cutting the price of its 2026-2027 Epic Passes for younger skiers and snowboarders.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times
item.media:description: Younger skiers and snowboarders, like this group at Keystone, in Colorado, will pay less for next season’s Epic Passes.

item.title: Apalachee High School Shooting: Colin Gray Found Guilty of Murder
item.description: A jury found that Colin Gray, the father of the suspect, bore criminal responsibility for the attack at a Georgia school by failing to heed warnings of his son’s struggles.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas and Johnny Kauffman
item.media:credit: Abbey Cutrer/Atlanta Journal-Constitution, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Colin Gray, the father of the suspect in the Apalachee High School shooting, during his trial in Winder, Ga.

item.title: Columbia Is Investigated for Handling of Sex-Abuse Claims Against Doctor
item.description: Attorney General Letitia James is investigating the university for its actions after the 2012 arrest of Robert Hadden, a former Columbia gynecologist convicted of sex crimes.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: About 1,000 women have accused Robert Hadden of sexually assaulting them during medical exams between 1993 and 2012.
